summary: Jeff Atwood shares practical techniques for speeding up web browsing by disabling Flash and using a custom HOSTS file to block advertising domains. He walks through the steps to disable Flash in Internet Explorer, noting that Flash is mostly decorative 'chrome' that slows down browsing and causes memory leaks in Firefox. The post then explains how the HOSTS file can redirect ad domain requests to localhost, returning instant 404 errors instead of downloading bulky ad content. Atwood frames these optimizations as pro-speed rather than anti-advertising, emphasizing that the fastest downloads are ones you never make.
key_points: Disabling Flash removes visual distractions and reduces page load times, since Flash content is usually decorative rather than essential
Flash causes significant memory leaks in Firefox, making it worth disabling for browser stability
The HOSTS file can be used to redirect ad domains to 127.0.0.1, blocking ads at the network level before DNS lookup
This approach returns 404 errors in milliseconds instead of downloading 20-50kb ad files from remote servers
The techniques are framed as performance optimizations rather than ideological opposition to advertising
money_quotes: I've got nothing personal against Flash, mind you, but it's generally chrome.
I rarely need to see Flash movies as part of my normal web browsing.
I'm not anti advertising so much as I am pro speed.
And the fastest downloads of all are the ones you don't have to do!
When your browser tries to download an advertising image from, say, m.doubleclick.net, it returns in under a millisecond with a 404 'file not found' error.
key_insight: The fastest way to speed up web browsing is to eliminate unnecessary downloads entirely, whether that's decorative Flash content or advertising files.
